schools.summary: Augustine on Luke's narrative order (recapitulating the morning); Bede: Christ delivered to the Gentiles (the Roman Pilate) fulfilling his prophecy (Luke 18:32) — the Sanhedrin lacking power to execute (John 18:31), handing him to Rome; both Jew and Gentile sharing in his death, the providence overruling malice to fulfil the Scriptures.

themes: the transition to the civil trial

schools.summary: Lightfoot: 'perverting the nation' a rabbinic phrase for mingling heresy with truth, turning the people from obedience; Barnes: 'this fellow' conveys contempt; the accusers shift from blasphemy (which would not move Pilate) to false political charges calculated to alarm Rome — Christ had commanded tribute (Luke 20:25), his kingship not of this world (John 18:36).

Robertson: all four Gospels give Pilate's question identically; Ellicott: 'Thou sayest it' a formula of confession (as Luke 22:70), to be read with John's fuller account; Christ openly owns his kingship, yet (John 18:36) of a kingdom not of this world, no rival to Caesar — the true King confessing his title before the earthly judge.

themes: art thou the King of the Jews
a kingdom not of this world

schools.summary: Gill: Pilate's second coming out, after private examination; Barnes: satisfied that Christ's kingdom was not of this world and no threat to Caesar (John 18:33-38), Pilate declares 'I find no fault' — the Gentile judge himself attesting Christ's innocence (the first of several acquittals), establishing that he died not as a malefactor but as the spotless sacrifice (Hebrews 7:26; 1 Peter 2:22).

themes: I find no fault in this man

schools.summary: Gill: more urgent and clamorous; Barnes: seeing their cause failing, they pressed the sedition charge — Christ 'stirreth up the people' throughout the land, from Galilee to Jerusalem, calculated to alarm Pilate; the persistence of malice redoubling as it seems to lose, the false charge of sedition, the providential mention of Galilee sending Christ to Herod.

Bede: Pilate, knowing Christ innocent and delivered for envy, was glad of an opportunity to escape passing judgment, sending him to Herod; Theophylact: following the Roman law that a man be judged by his own province's governor; the cowardice of Pilate, knowing the right but fearing to do it, seeking to shift responsibility — the prelude to his fall.

themes: he asked whether the man were a Galilaean
the pretext to send him to Herod

schools.summary: Gill: Herod Antipas (John's murderer), tetrarch of Galilee; Pilate's double motive — to be rid of the case and to honour Herod's jurisdiction; Lightfoot on the deference shown; Christ shuttled from judge to judge, the providence fulfilling Psalm 2 (the kings and rulers gathered against the Lord, Acts 4:26-27), Pilate's evasion widening the circle of guilt.

themes: the rulers gathered against the Lord

schools.summary: Gregory: Herod wished to witness Christ's miracles; Theophylact: not for benefit but seized with curiosity; Ellicott: the wonder begun after John's death, mingled with bitter enmity no miracle could remove; Herod's idle curiosity — seeking Christ as a wonder-worker to gratify himself, not as Saviour — the barren approach that finds no saving response.

themes: seeking Christ for wonders not salvation

schools.summary: Gill: Herod pressed him to work a miracle; Barnes: the silence fulfilling prophecy (Isaiah 53:7); Ellicott: the impressive contrast — Christ answered Caiaphas's solemn adjuration but was silent before Herod's idle question; the majestic silence of the Lamb, refusing to be a spectacle, a judgment on Herod's frivolity and hardness — Christ reveals himself to faith, not curiosity.

Gill: the Sanhedrin followed Christ to Herod's court, fearing he might release him; Clarke: corrupt priests the most implacable enemies of Christ, their vehemence betraying envy (which seldom consults prudence) — the relentless malice pursuing Christ from court to court, the very fury exposing the falseness of their cause, contrasting his calm silence.

Barnes: the 'gorgeous robe' a white/shining kingly garment (Jewish royal robe made splendid with silver), decking Christ in mockery of his kingship; Gill: 'his men of war' Herod's bodyguard; Herod's curiosity, disappointed by Christ's silence, souring into contempt — mocking the true King of kings as a false king, the meek Lamb enduring the scorn, Herod's sending-back a confession of no crime.

themes: set him at nought and mocked him

schools.summary: Gill: the reconciliation from mutual courtesy (Pilate honouring Herod's jurisdiction, Herod referring back); Barnes: the prior quarrel's cause unknown (perhaps Luke 13:1), and — against the common reading — not mere shared hatred, since Pilate wished to release Christ; the former enemies made friends over the trial, fulfilling Psalm 2 (the rulers gathered against the Lord, Acts 4:27).

debates.question: Why were Pilate and Herod reconciled?

sides.position: From mutual courtesy over the jurisdiction, not simple shared hatred (Pilate wished to release Christ)
United in common opposition to Christ (the kings gathered against the Lord, Psalm 2)

themes: the rulers gathered against the Lord

schools.summary: Augustine: Luke returns to the proceedings before Pilate; Ambrose: Pilate, who as judge acquits Christ, is made the minister of his crucifixion, gratifying the Jews for fear's sake; Theophylact: by the two judges' testimony (Pilate and Herod) Jesus is doubly declared innocent — truth triumphing through the trial, the tragic cowardice of the judge who acquits yet condemns.

themes: the judge who acquits yet condemns

schools.summary: Robertson: Pilate condenses the three charges (v.2) into one (treason, a dangerous rival to Caesar), and 'having examined him before you' = a careful public examination; Pilate's formal, public acquittal — no fault touching the charges — made before the accusers, leaving the rulers without excuse and establishing on the record that Christ died as the innocent, the spotless sacrifice.

themes: I have found no fault in this man

schools.summary: Gill: Herod, a Jew versed in their affairs, also found no fault; Barnes: tried before two judges with every opportunity to prove the charges, Christ declared innocent by both — 'no better evidence that he was innocent'; the double acquittal (Pilate and Herod, of differing nations and dispositions) setting Christ's guiltlessness beyond dispute — the spotless Lamb condemned in our stead.

Gill: Pilate proposed scourging not as deserved but in complaisance to the Jews, to make their charges seem weighty and hope it sufficient to save Christ; Barnes: to scourge the innocent was as unjust as to crucify — the fatal compromise of the judge who will not do full justice, his half-measure inviting further demand and drawing him on to the cross.

themes: I will chastise him and release him

schools.summary: Clarke: the fixed Passover custom of releasing one prisoner, which Pilate was obliged to observe; Ellicott: the verse absent from many of the best MSS, likely an explanatory gloss that entered the text — though the custom itself is firm in Matthew 27:15 and Mark 15:6; the custom framing the fatal choice between Christ and Barabbas, the substance secure whatever the verse's textual status.

debates.question: Is Luke 23:17 part of the original text?

sides.position: Likely a later explanatory gloss (absent from many best MSS), though the custom is firm in Matthew/Mark

themes: he must release one at the feast
the framing of the fatal choice

schools.summary: Robertson: 'all together' (a rare adverb), 'Away with him' (take him away and keep him away, cf. Acts 21:36); Gill: they would not hear of Christ's release; the whole multitude with one voice preferring Barabbas the murderer to the innocent Christ — the perversity and unanimity of rejection, the providential exchange (the guilty freed, the innocent condemned) picturing the gospel.

themes: the guilty freed, the innocent condemned

schools.summary: Robertson: Barabbas guilty of the very insurrection (untrue of Jesus) and murder — had Jesus raised insurrection, these accusers would have rallied to him; the murderer counted a hero by some; Ellicott: John calls him a robber, Matthew a notable prisoner; the bitter irony of the real criminal preferred to the innocent Christ — the gospel figure of the guilty freed because Christ bore his cross (Luke 22:37).

themes: the bitter irony of the exchange

schools.summary: Gill: Pilate, more convinced of Christ's innocence, seeing the accusers' malice, and warned by his wife's dream (Matthew 27:19), spoke again willing to release him; the genuine struggle in Pilate between known justice and fear of the crowd — good intentions without the courage to act, the weakness that would yet fail to do the right he desired.

Robertson: the imperfect 'they kept on yelling,' the present imperative 'go on with the crucifixion'; Gill: fiercer the more Pilate sought to save him; the crowd demanding not merely death but crucifixion (the cruelest, most shameful death) — the rejection of Christ at its most violent, the providence overruling the cry to fulfil the appointed death of the cross (John 12:32-33; Psalm 22:16).

themes: the cry overruled to fulfil the cross

schools.summary: Clarke: Christ formally justified by his judge, publicly and perhaps on record — established that he died innocent, not a malefactor, on the fullest conviction of his judge; the threefold acquittal (vv.4, 14-15, 22) leaving no doubt of the Lamb's guiltlessness (1 Peter 2:22); Pilate's persistent weakness, thrice proposing the unjust compromise, conviction without courage.

themes: I find no cause of death

schools.summary: Robertson: 'they pressed upon him' (the rush of a tempest), Pilate overwhelmed by the tornado he had invited; Ellicott: 'instant' = urgent; the loud voices of the crowd and chief priests prevailing over Pilate's weak resistance — justice overborne not by argument but by clamour and persistence, the judge without courage swept away, the providence overruling to accomplish the appointed death.

Cyril: the disgraceful malady of folly of heart, led by base thoughts to sin against the glory of God; Gill: Pilate sentenced according to their request; the surrender of justice to the mob — Pilate, who thrice acquitted Christ, condemning him not by proof but by the crowd's demand (mob law by the judge), the path of cowardice ending in the sentence of the innocent, overruled to accomplish redemption.

themes: the folly of heart condemning Christ

schools.summary: Gill: Barabbas unnamed as too detestable; Robertson: 'to their will' = mob law, the judge surrendering the innocent to the crowd's malice; the exchange completed — the murderer freed, Christ delivered up — the figure of the gospel (the guilty set free because the innocent is delivered for our offences, Romans 4:25), the Lamb given over yet by the determinate counsel of God (Acts 2:23).

themes: the guilty freed, the innocent condemned

schools.summary: Augustine harmonizes with John — Christ bore the cross first, then Simon was compelled on the road (his strength failing); Theophylact: the cross an abomination none would bear, laid on Simon to his dishonour, yet fulfilling 'the government upon his shoulder' (Isaiah 9:6) — the cross Christ's reign; Simon the type of cross-bearing discipleship (Luke 9:23), tradition holding he became a believer (Mark 15:21).

Ellicott: the mourning women a detail peculiar to Luke (drawn from the devout women), the 'daughters of Jerusalem' perhaps a sisterhood for the condemned; Clarke: 'bewailed and lamented' = beat their breasts; the tender compassion of the women conspicuous amid the cruelty, their grief real yet (as Christ would teach) partly misdirected — to be turned from his sufferings to their own coming judgment.

Barnes: 'weep for yourselves' = the coming desolation of Jerusalem by the Romans (AD 70), on the women and their children; Clarke: Christ's sufferings expiatory, borne by God in the flesh — no man can share them, so not to be merely pitied but received; Christ's selfless compassion turning their tears from his sufferings to their own coming judgment, redirecting sentiment to repentance over sin.

themes: weep not for me but for yourselves

schools.summary: Ellicott/Robertson: 'blessed are the barren' overturns the deepest desire of Israelite women (childlessness the bitterest grief, Genesis 30:1; 1 Samuel 1:10-11) — the measure of the coming horror; Calvin: a calamity fearful and unheard of; the destruction of Jerusalem (AD 70) so extreme that barrenness becomes a mercy — the judgment for rejecting the Messiah, Christ's compassion warning even those who slew him.

Gill: the siege so terrible (per Josephus) that many sought the caves, preferring death; Robertson: a quotation from Hosea 10:8; Lightfoot connects it to Revelation 6:16 (the cry of the wicked at the day of wrath) — the doomed begging the mountains to crush them, death sought as relief; the cry of Jerusalem prefiguring the cry of all the impenitent under final judgment.

Gill: the 'green tree' is Christ — innocent, full of grace, not fit for burning yet so treated; the 'dry tree' the guilty nation fit for the fire; the argument from lesser to greater — if the innocent Christ suffered so, far worse awaits the guilty (cf. 1 Peter 4:17-18); the destruction of Jerusalem the judgment on the dry tree, Christ warning the daughters of Jerusalem of the coming doom.

debates.question: What are the green tree and the dry?

sides.position: The green tree = the innocent Christ; the dry = the guilty nation fit for judgment

themes: the green tree and the dry
if they do these things to the innocent
the argument from lesser to greater

schools.summary: Cyril connects the two malefactors to Isaiah 53:12 (numbered with the transgressors), which Christ cited (Luke 22:37); the innocent Lamb crucified between two criminals, reckoned among the guilty — the depth of his humiliation and the sign of his substitution (made sin for us), the cross between which he hung becoming the dividing line of faith and unbelief in the two thieves.

Athanasius: Christ crucified at Calvary (held to be Adam's burial-place) that incorruption might spring where corruption had been — the second Adam undoing the first's death at the spot; Bede: Calvary the 'place of skulls' where criminals died, the innocent crucified among the guilty that grace might abound where sin abounded (Romans 5:20) — the cross the throne of saving grace, Christ in the midst dividing the two thieves.

Chrysostom: Christ practises his own teaching (Matthew 5:44), praying for his persecutors in deed not word only; Bede: the prayer for those who acted in ignorance (a zeal without knowledge), not those who knowingly crucified the Son of God, answered in those who believed after (Acts 2:37-41); the love of Christ interceding for his murderers (Isaiah 53:12; Hebrews 7:25), the parting of garments fulfilling Psalm 22:18.

debates.question: For whom did Christ pray 'forgive them'?

sides.position: For those who acted in ignorance (soldiers, the misled people), not those who knowingly crucified the Son of God
A pattern of praying for all persecutors, answered in those who later believed

themes: for they know not what they do

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'derided' implies the curled lip of scorn, and 'He saved others' an unconscious confession (like Caiaphas, John 11:50); Gill: the rulers, who should have been restrained, joined the insult; the taunt 'let him save himself' assumed his staying proved him no Christ — but he did not save himself because he was saving others, the cross the place of victory, not failure.

themes: he saved others, let him save himself
he saved others, therefore not himself

schools.summary: Lightfoot: vinegar the common drink of Roman soldiers, kept ready by the guards, offered in mockery; Gill: the executioner-soldiers joining the insult; the scorn now universal — Jew and Gentile, ruler and soldier (fulfilling Psalm 22:6-8) — the King mocked, the soldiers' vinegar fulfilling Psalm 69:21; the patience of Christ enduring the contempt of all in silence.

Robertson: the soldiers pick out 'king of the Jews' (the point of condemnation) as their sneer, failing to grasp that Jesus could not save himself if he was to save others; Gill: daring him as their king to deliver himself; the mockery of the true King whose throne was the cross — his staying not weakness but the work of redemption, the kingship won by self-giving, not self-deliverance.

themes: if thou be the king of the Jews
could not save himself and others both

schools.summary: Theophylact: the title in three tongues (Greek, Latin, Hebrew) overruled the devil's device — proclaiming that the most powerful (Romans), wisest (Greeks), and most religious (Jews) must be subject to Christ; Ambrose: rightly placed above the cross, for Christ's kingdom is of God's power not the body ('My kingdom is not of this world,' John 18:36) — the charge turned into a universal proclamation of his kingship.

themes: THIS IS THE KING OF THE JEWS

schools.summary: Gill/Barnes reconcile with Matthew/Mark (both railed at first, one later converted, or the plural loose); Barnes exposes the railing thief's heart — 'save thyself and us' sought deliverance from the cross, not from sin (men in affliction crying only for relief, not pardon); the hardness that perishes unrepentant even at death's door, contrasting the penitent — the difference being grace, not circumstance.

debates.question: How are Matthew/Mark (both thieves railed) and Luke (one penitent) reconciled?

sides.position: Both railed at first, one afterward converted; or the plural is used loosely

themes: one of the malefactors railed on him

schools.summary: Barnes: 'dost not thou fear God, seeing thou art in the same condemnation' — both dying, hastening to God's bar, so he should fear; Clarke: the penitent's sufferings sanctified, a genuine penitent giving the fullest proof — the fear of God the first fruit of conversion (Proverbs 9:10); a dying criminal regenerated, boldly rebuking the railing and confessing Christ before mockers.

Gill: true sense of sin acknowledges the offence and vindicates God's justice ('we indeed justly'); Ellicott: his confidence in Christ's innocence from prior knowledge, report, or Pilate's acquittals; Barnes: 'nothing amiss' echoes the Lamb without blemish (1 Peter 1:19) — the twofold mark of repentance, owning his own just guilt and Christ's spotless innocence, the broken heart justifying God.

themes: this man hath done nothing amiss

schools.summary: Gill: the thief's faith surpassed the apostles' — owning the crucified Christ as 'Lord' and King, believing his kingdom spiritual not of this world (where the apostles looked for a temporal one), believing he would enter glory through death and come again; faith piercing the deepest humiliation to behold the King, the humble plea to be 'remembered' — a marvel of sovereign grace at the point of death.

themes: when thou comest into thy kingdom
beholding the King in the crucified

schools.summary: Gill: Christ answered the penitent (not the railer), granting more than asked and sooner than expected; Lightfoot: 'paradise' = the garden of Eden, the abode of the blessed dead; the immediacy of 'to day' — the believer's soul at death passing at once into the conscious presence of Christ (Philippians 1:23; 2 Corinthians 5:8), not sleep nor long waiting; the riches and freeness of grace saving a dying thief at the last hour.

themes: to day shalt thou be with me in paradise
the riches and freeness of grace

schools.summary: Cyril: the darkness creation mourning its Master, a token that the crucifiers' souls would suffer darkness; Augustine: no natural eclipse (the Passover full moon, opposite to an eclipse's new moon) but supernatural; Dionysius reports the marvel at Heliopolis; the three hours' midday darkness a divine portent — the heavens veiling their face at the Maker's death, a sign of judgment on the guilty nation.

debates.question: Was the darkness a natural eclipse?

sides.position: No — impossible at the Passover full moon (eclipses occur only at new moon); a supernatural portent

themes: the sixth to the ninth hour

schools.summary: Robertson: 'the sun's light failing' (the textual variant 'darkened' to avoid the eclipse term, impossible at full moon); Gill: the darkness preternatural and total; the veil rent 'in the midst' (top to bottom, Matthew 27:51) — the way into the holiest opened by Christ's death (Hebrews 10:19-20), the end of the old order and ceremonial law, God's own hand declaring the sacrifice accepted and access now free.

themes: the veil of the temple was rent
the way into the holiest opened
the end of the old order

schools.summary: Clarke: 'into thy hands I commend my spirit' (Psalm 31:5) proves the soul's immateriality and separate existence when the body dies; Ellicott: 'he breathed out his spirit,' the loud cry immediately preceding death; the trustful surrender of Christ to the Father (the pattern for the believer, Acts 7:59), and the loud cry a sign of unspent strength — the death voluntary, laid down of himself (John 10:18).

themes: Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit

schools.summary: Augustine reconciles the accounts: Luke's 'what was done' (Christ's power in dying, the loud cry, the signs) and 'a righteous man' with Matthew/Mark's 'Truly the Son of God' — the centurion confessing both; the Gentile soldier, charged with the execution, glorifying God and owning Christ righteous and divine where the rulers mocked — the first fruits of the Gentiles, the converting power of the cross.

themes: certainly this was a righteous man
the converting power of the cross

schools.summary: Barnes: the people smote their breasts in alarm, seeing the judgments of God and the rulers' guilt, fearing further wrath; Clarke: all deeply affected (save the priests), the darkness and earthquake bringing terror to every heart; the spectacle-crowd convicted by the signs of Christ's death — a stricken conscience, a beginning ripening at Pentecost (Acts 2:37), contrasting the hardened rulers.

themes: the convicting power of the cross

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'acquaintance' (used only here) intermediate between spectators and avowed disciples (secret believers, or those who served him); the women unnamed here (named in Luke 8:2-3), who followed from Galilee and ministered; Barnes: Psalm 38:11 (friends afar off); the faithful watchers standing by when the apostles fled — the women's devotion conspicuous, last at the cross, first at the tomb.

themes: love that endured to the end

schools.summary: The Greek Expositor: Joseph a secret disciple who burst through fear to take down Christ's body; Bede: 'counsellor' (decurio) of the council, of high rank in the world but higher before God — good and just, fitted by his righteousness for the burial; the providence raising up a friend in the enemy's camp, grace ripening timid faith into open courage when the cause seemed lost (John 19:38).

themes: a good man and a just

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph, though of the council, did not consent to their condemnation of Christ; Ellicott distinguishes 'the counsel' (the official action) from 'the deed' (the bargain with Judas, the delivery to Pilate), Joseph apart from all; he 'waited for the kingdom of God' (with Simeon and Anna, Luke 2:25, 38) — the integrity standing apart from the crowd's crime, and the hope of the kingdom marking the true disciple.

themes: had not consented to their deed
waited for the kingdom of God
standing apart from the council's crime

schools.summary: Robertson: the middle voice shows Joseph asked the body as a personal favour, and Mark says he went 'boldly' (15:43); Joseph, the once-secret disciple, openly approaching Pilate at great risk (confessing himself a follower of the condemned) — the courage of love in the darkest hour, the providence securing an honourable burial (Isaiah 53:9), grace turning fear into bold confession (John 19:38).

Bede: the lesson from the simple burial and the fitness of the new tomb; the body wrapped in fine linen (with Nicodemus's spices, John 19:39-40) and laid in a rock-hewn sepulchre 'wherein never man was laid' — an honourable burial fulfilling Isaiah 53:9 ('with the rich in his death'); the virgin tomb (answering the virgin womb) ensuring no confusion at the resurrection, the careful burial confirming his death.

Gill: the preparation day (for the sabbath and the festival); Lightfoot: 'the sabbath drew on' (lit. 'began to dawn'), the Jewish day beginning at sundown with the sabbath candles; the burial hurried before the sabbath (leaving the full anointing for after) — the providence ordering Christ's death and burial on the preparation, rest over the sabbath, resurrection on the first day, fulfilling the sign of Jonah (Matthew 12:40).

Ellicott: traces of Luke's informants — the women from Galilee who saw the cross, the body taken down, and followed to the tomb, their observation the source of Luke's detail; Clarke names them (Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Mary of James, Luke 24:10); the faithful women marking exactly where and how Christ was laid, that they might return with spices — sure witnesses of both the burial and the empty tomb.

themes: marking how his body was laid
sure witnesses of burial and resurrection

schools.summary: Lightfoot: the women had the interval before the sabbath to buy spices ('between the suns'), then rested; Gill: they rested the sabbath 'according to the commandment'; the union of love and obedience — preparing spices to anoint Christ yet keeping the sabbath rest, reverence for God's law governing their devotion; their rest bringing them to the tomb on the first day (Luke 24:1), the first witnesses of the resurrection.
